hypothesis: Incorporating calibrated predictive uncertainty into the physics-based reward for Direct Preference Optimization—via an ensemble or Bayesian CALPHAD/Thermo-Calc surrogate that yields a reward distribution rather than a single scalar and training the LM with a risk-sensitive objective—will improve robustness and generalization of LM-driven BCC/B2 alloy design, increasing synthesis-viability (meeting all four synthesis criteria) and design-space exploration in unseen multi-element compositions while reducing drift on domain-critical tokens across LM architectures compared to deterministic Thermo-Calc rewards.
